coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive quantitative detection of glutathione based on a fluorescence-colorimetric dual signal recognition strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiansen
Cao, Chen
Li, Huan
Chen, Shutong
Gong, Xiaoqun
Wang, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) serves as a critical component in numerous physiological processes, functioning as a significant antioxidant. Therefore, the establishment of a precise method for the quantification of GSH is of paramount significance for disease diagnosis, clinical interventions, and various biomedical applications. In this study, a nanocomposite material (NSCQDs@MSN) is prepared by in situ synthesizing nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) in mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSN). By loading Cu2+ on the surface of NSCQDs@MSN, a novel dual-mode sensor (NSCQDs@MSN-Cu) is developed for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of GSH. On the one hand, based on the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by the electron or energy transfer process between Cu2+ and NSCQDs, a "Turn-On" fluorescence sensing model is successfully constructed for GSH detection; on the other hand, based on the principle that NSCQDs@MSN-Cu can catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by hydrogen peroxide to produce blue oxTMB, a colorimetric sensing model is devised for the detection of GSH. This dual-mode sensor was successfully used for the detection of GSH in real samples, and the experimental results further verified the accuracy and practicality of the dual signal recognition strategy. Thus, this strategy not only introduces a novel research concept for the creation of multifunctional sensors but also holds significant promise for early disease diagnosis and biochemical research applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Glutathione
Dual-mode
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GSH in real cell samples
GSH detection strategy of the dual-mode sensor system
Analytical performance of the dual-mode sensor
Specificity and stability of the dual-mode sensing system for GSH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and compositional characterizations of MSN and NSCQDs@MSN-Cu. (a) Representative low-resolution TEM image of MSN. Scale bar: 500 nm. (b) Representative high-resolution TEM images of MSN. Scale bar: 200 nm. (c) The hydrodynamic size distribution histogram of MSN. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of NSCQDs@MSN. (e) STEM and corresponding elemental mapping of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu.

Steady-state kinetic of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu. (a, b) Michaelis-Menten kinetic analysis (a) and Lineweaver-Burk plot (b) for NSCQDs@MSN-Cu with H2O2 as a substrate. (c, d) Michaelis-Menten kinetic analysis (c) and Lineweaver-Burk plot (d) for NSCQDs@MSN-Cu with TMB as a substrate. (e) Absorbance changes at 652 nm versus time for the TMB + H2O2 system using Cu2+, NSCQDs@MSN and NSCQDs@MSN-Cu.

(a) Schematic illustration of the reactions. (b) Fluorescence responses detection system. (c) Colorimetric responses detection system. Fluorescence and absorbance responses intensity the dual-mode sensor at different adsorbed Cu2+ concentration (d), test solution pH (e), and NSCQDs@MSN-Cu concentration (f).

Analytical performance of dual-mode sensor for GSH detection. (a)Fluorescence signal intensity change of developed NSCQDs@MSN-Cu sensor versus the different concentrations of GSH. (b) The linear relationship between the signal intensity acquired by the Fluorescence mode and the GSH concentration. (c, f) Selectivity and anti-interference performance of the constructed dual-mode sensing platform. (interfering substances contain Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, AA, Ala, Cys, and Glu). (d) Absorbance signal intensity changeat at 652 nm of developed NSCQDs@MSN-Cu sensor versus the different concentrations of GSH. (e) The linear relationship between the signal intensity acquired by the Colorimetric mode and the GSH concentration. (g) Stability and repeatability of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu sensor at 4°C environment.

Visual detection of dual-mode sensor toward GSH. (a) Colors change of detection system versus the concentrations of GSH. (b) Scheme diagram of the smartphone-based sensor for GSH monitoring. (c) The linear relationship between the color intensity acquired by the RGB mode and the GSH concentration.
Schematic illustration showing the synthesis of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu and its applications for the dual signal recognition detection.
Comparison of dual-mode sensor and other reported sensors for GSH determination.
Detection result of GSH in the actual sample (cell lysate).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive quantitative detection of glutathione based on a fluorescence-colorimetric dual signal recognition strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Function and Application of Biological Macromolecular Structures, School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Function and Application of Biological Macromolecular Structures, School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Function and Application of Biological Macromolecular Structures, School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) serves as a critical component in numerous physiological processes, functioning as a significant antioxidant. Therefore, the establishment of a precise method for the quantification of GSH is of paramount significance for disease diagnosis, clinical interventions, and various biomedical applications. In this study, a nanocomposite material (NSCQDs@MSN) is prepared by in situ synthesizing nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) in mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSN). By loading Cu2+ on the surface of NSCQDs@MSN, a novel dual-mode sensor (NSCQDs@MSN-Cu) is developed for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of GSH. On the one hand, based on the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by the electron or energy transfer process between Cu2+ and NSCQDs, a "Turn-On" fluorescence sensing model is successfully constructed for GSH detection; on the other hand, based on the principle that NSCQDs@MSN-Cu can catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by hydrogen peroxide to produce blue oxTMB, a colorimetric sensing model is devised for the detection of GSH. This dual-mode sensor was successfully used for the detection of GSH in real samples, and the experimental results further verified the accuracy and practicality of the dual signal recognition strategy. Thus, this strategy not only introduces a novel research concept for the creation of multifunctional sensors but also holds significant promise for early disease diagnosis and biochemical research applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: By loading Cu2+ on the surface of NSCQDs@MSN, a novel dual-mode sensor (NSCQDs@MSN-Cu) is developed for fluorescence and colorimetric detection of GSH.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a sulfhydryl-containing tripeptide compound composed of glycine, glutamic acid, and cysteine, widely present in every cell of the body [1–3]. Glutathione is divided into two intracellular forms, reduced glutathione and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) [4,5]. Under normal physiological conditions, reduced GSH accounts for the majority (＞90%) [6–8]. GSH is the most widely distributed natural antioxidant in the body, scavenging free radicals and preventing oxidation of sulfhydryl proteins and enzymes [9–11]. There is an intracellular GSH/GSSG conversion cycle, in which GSH can undergo oxidative reactions with intracellular peroxides and reactive oxygen radicals (ROS) [2,12–15], and be converted into GSSG; the GSSG can be reduced back to GSH in the presence of glutathione reductase [16]. Abnormal levels of GSH in the human body have been reported to be closely associated with a variety of disease-related pathological processes, including cancer [17], arthritis, neurodegenerative diseases and diabetes [18–20]. For example, the disparity in GSH concentration between cancerous cells and healthy cells serves as a crucial biochemical parameter in cancer diagnosis [5,21]; therefore, precise measurement of GSH levels is imperative for early disease detection and effective therapy [3,22].

In recent decades, a variety of GSH detection techniques [23,24], mainly including mass spectrometry [25], colorimetry [14,26], surface enhancement of Raman scattering [20], electrochemiluminescence [27], and fluorescence spectroscopy (FS) [11,15,28,29] have been developed. It is worth noting that although these assays can basically meet the needs and have unique advantages in detection [15,30], the analytical properties of assays based on a single signal mode in complex samples may be affected by multiple external factors (e.g., interfering substances, instrumental operation fluctuations, and background signals) [24,31], making it difficult to perform accurate detection [32–34]. Hence, there is a pressing demand for a multi-mode assay capable of quantitatively measuring GSH with a high degree of precision.

Multi-mode detection and recognition refers to the fusion of multiple recognition signals to utilize their multi-signal characteristics [30,35], making the detection and recognition process more accurate and stable [36,37]. This strategy is like a human olfactory or gustatory system that employs multiple cross-reactive detectors to produce a unique response pattern for each target, making the obtained results more accurate [38]. The key to multi-modal response detection is to obtain multiple signal outputs, which can be validated against each other to obtain more reliable results [28,39]. As a result, sensing systems that operate in "Turn-On" or "signal enhancement" modes are highly desirable because they are more sensitive and less likely to generate false positives [14,40,41].

In this study, by in situ synthesis of nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) in mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSN) and subsequent Cu2+ adsorption, a facile dual-mode sensor (NSCQDs@MSN-Cu) was prepared for ultrasensitive detection of GSH. The detection process is illustrated in Scheme 1, when GSH is introduced into the sensing system, Cu2+ is induced to detach from the surface of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu due to its stronger affinity with GSH. On the one hand, the detachment of Cu2+ will lead to the fluorescence recovery of NSCQDs, and based on the degree of fluorescence recovery, the fluorescence quantitative detection of GSH can be realized. On the other hand, by using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the chromogenic substrate, the binding of Cu2+ to GSH can both greatly reduce the efficiency of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu to decompose hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to oxidize TMB into oxTMB. Simultaneously, the oxidized product, oxTMB, can be re-reduced to colorless TMB due to the reducing property of GSH. Therefore, the colorimetric detection of GSH can be achieved through the observable change in the blue signal, which is discernible to the naked eye. This dual-mode signal detection method offers crucial advantages, including a short detection time, high sensitivity, versatile detection modes, and immunity to interference from other ions and amino acids. It holds significant promise for application in the field of biosensing.

3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 99%), L-Alanine (L-Ala), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and triethanolamine (TEA, 99%) were obtained from Meryer (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, KCl, and NaCl were bought from Tianjin Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), Cysteine (Cys), Glutamate (Glu), and Glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Heowns Biochem Technologies LLC (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm at 25°C) from a LS MK2 PALL-water purification system was used in all experiments with aqueous solutions.

UV–vis spectra were measured by UV–vis absorption spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450, Japan). The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential were measured by laser particle size analyzer (Malvern, Zetasizer nano ZS90). The morphology was characterized by transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2F20). The fluorescence curves were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-2500, Japan).

Homogeneous-sized MSN was synthesized by self-assembly in an oil-water two-phase layered reaction system using CTAB as a structure-directing agent and TEOS as a silica precursor [42,43]. In brief, 60 mL of ultrapure water, 100 μL of TEA solution, and 3 g of CTAB were added to a 150 mL three-neck flask. The mixed solution was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 10 min (500 rpm) until the mixed solution was clarified, and then continuously stirred at 60°C. After that, 16 mL of TEOS and 16 mL of cyclohexane were added to the top of the above mixed solution to form a biphasic phase. After 48 h of reaction, the MSN was washed several times with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water.

Next, NSCQDs were synthesized by a one-step microwave method [44,45]: 2 g of citric acid and 0.4 g of cysteine were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and 50 mg of MSN was added homogeneously under stirring conditions. The resulting mixed solution was then reacted at 700 W microwave for 5 min, and it could be observed that the mixed solution changed from a pale white liquid to a brown colloid, indicating the formation of NSCQDs. The colloid obtained from the reaction was then dispersed in 5 mL of ultrapure water and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove the incompletely reacted precursor molecules. Finally, the obtained NSCQDs@MSN were washed twice with ethanol and dissolved in 2 mL of ultrapure water and stored at 4℃ for subsequent experiments.

The synthesized NSCQDs@MSN was dispersed in 8 mL of ultrapure water for sufficient dispersion. CuSO4 solution (1 M, 2 mL) was added to the above solution and then stirred continuously for 4 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with ultrapure water until the supernatant was nearly colorless. Finally, the NSCQDs@MSN-Cu was obtained.

The kinetic experiments were performed to measure alterations in absorbance values at 652 nm by varying the TMB concentration and fixing the H2O2 concentration, or vice versa. The specific method is as follows: Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed in PBS buffers (0.01 M, pH 7.4) containing different concentrations of TMB (0.25 ∼ 4 mM). To the above solution, NSCQDs@MSN-Cu (20 μL, 1.1 mg/mL) and 5 mM H2O2 were added to a total volume of 2 mL, and the absorbance magnitude of different concentrations of TMB was recorded using time scanning (652 nm). Under the same conditions, the TMB concentration (2.4 mM) was fixed and the H2O2 concentration (0.5 ∼ 6 mM) was varied for the next step of analysis. The values of kinetic parameters Km and Vmax were calculated using the Michaelis-Menten equation and Lineweaver-Burk plot:
V = Vmax [S] / (Km + [S]), where V and Vmax represent the initial and maximum initial reaction rates, respectively. [S] is the substrate (TMB or H2O2) concentration and Km is Michaelis constant.

The dual-mode detection of GSH was realized by the following steps: Initially, the fluorescence detection system was obtained by adding NSCQDs@MSN-Cu (20 μL, 1.1 mg/mL) to 2 mL of PBS buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 7.0). Subsequently, concentrations of GSH were added to the above mixed solution and reacted for 3 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were scanned in the range of 370 nm ∼ 550 nm and the fluorescence intensities at 416 nm were recorded. Following this, the colorimetric sensing system was constructed under the same conditions, a certain amount of H2O2 (0.5 mM) and TMB (5 mM) solutions were added, and the colorimetric analysis was performed by the absorbance signal at 652 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in real cell samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the dual-mode sensor in a real-world detection environment, mouse breast cancer (4T1) cells and colorectal cancer (CT26) cells, and human-lung cells (A549) lysates were utilized as real samples for sensor performance evaluation. A sample of cell lysate was first prepared. Cultured cells were collected and washed 2 ∼ 3 times using serum-free culture media. Then, according to the number of cells, the cell lysis buffer was added and allowed to come into complete contact with the cells and react until were no visible cell particles. Finally, the lysate underwent centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was transferred to a sterile tube for GSH content determination, alongside a spiked recovery assay.

The outcomes of the incremental synthesis process for NSCQDs@MSN-Cu were characterized and assessed via techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. As shown in the TEM images ( Fig. 1a and b), the synthesized MSNs exhibited a well-organized pore structure with an approximate pore size of 12 nm. This mesoporous configuration can substantially enhance substrate contact, thereby promoting reaction occurrence. The particle size of MSN was statistically analyzed to be about 155.7 ± 11.6 nm (Fig. 1c). The UV-Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of NSCQDs@MSN were shown in Fig. 1d. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum (blue solid line) showed that the absorption bands of NSCQDs@MSN were mainly located in the wavelength range of 260 ∼ 350 nm, with a typical absorption peak at 348 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* leaps of the CC bond and the n-π* leaps of the CO bond. The fluorescence emission spectrum (red solid line) of NSCQDs@MSN showed the strongest fluorescence emission peak at 416 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. This result proved that NSCQDs were successfully in situ synthesized in MSN by the one-step microwave method. Uniformly distributed elements of C, N, S, Si, and Cu can be observed from the corresponding elemental mapping of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu (Fig. 1e). These findings confirmed the successful adsorption of Cu2+ on MSN and the successful construction of the intended target product. In addition, the morphology of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu was similar to that of MSN, verifying that the loading of Cu2+ did not change the structure of MSN.

All kinetic experiments were conducted in a 2 mL PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4), with the inclusion of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu (20 μL, 1.1 mg/mL). In these experiments, one of the concentrations was maintained constant, either H2O2 (ranging from 0.5 to 6 mM) or TMB (ranging from 0.25 to 4 mM), while the other concentration was varied. The Michaelis-Menten fitting plots of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu to different substrates were obtained by measuring the corresponding initial reaction rates and substrate concentrations ( Fig. 2a and c). Taking the inverse of the transformed form of the data in Fig. 2a and c, the transformed yielded Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. 2b and d), where it was clearly found that there was a good linear relationship between the inverse of the substrate concentration and the inverse of the rate, and the steady-state kinetic parameters (Km,Vmax) were computed from the Michaelis-Menten formula. The kinetic parameters of the reported peroxidase mimics are given in Table 1. The results showed that the Km values with NSCQDs@MSN-Cu with H2O2 as substrate were much less than those of horseradish peroxidase, indicating a strong affinity between NSCQDs@MSN-Cu and H2O2 and excellent catalytic ability.

Furthermore, the enhancement of peroxidase-like activity was confirmed via UV–vis analysis (refer to Fig. 2e). Given that Cu2+ exhibits peroxidase-like properties, it catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2, leading to the generation of reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH). The •OH led to the oxidation of TMB, resulting in the distinct color transformation of TMB into oxTMB. To examine this, Cu2+, NSCQDs@MSN, and NSCQDs @MSN-Cu were introduced into 2 mL PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4) containing equivalent concentrations of H2O2 and TMB for the reaction. The results showed that the absorbance signal at 652 nm of the NSCQDs@MSN-Cu system was significantly enhanced compared with that of Cu2+ alone, which could be attributed to the ability of MSN to selectively enrich Cu2+ and its own specific mesoporous structure to accelerate the electron transfer between Cu2+ and H2O2 to improve the catalytic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH detection strategy of the dual-mode sensor system
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the above proposed detection mechanism, a feasibility study was carried out under different experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 3b, NSCQDs@MSN exhibited a strong fluorescence emission peak at 416 nm (curve a). With the addition of Cu2+, NSCQDs@MSN-Cu were formed. The effective electron or energy transfer process between Cu2+ and NSCQDs led to an obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the NSCQDs, and the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced (curve b). However, with the addition of GSH, due to the high affinity of GSH for Cu2+, GSH could coordinate with Cu2+ in NSCQDs@MSN-Cu, leading to the fluorescence recovery of NSCQDs (curve c). Therefore, based on the above sensing strategy process, a "Turn-On" type fluorescence sensing model was successfully constructed for GSH detection. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 3c, since NSCQDs@MSN-Cu could catalyze the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate TMB to produce the blue oxTMB, a large absorption peak appeared at 652 nm (curves a-b). The subsequent addition of GSH could cause fade of the blue solution and decrease of the absorbance (curve c). In accordance with this principle, a set of colorimetric sensing modes based on the interconversion of the oxidized and reduced states of TMB were established for the detection of GSH. The above results further validated the mechanistic feasibility of the constructed dual-mode sensing system.

Typically, the effectiveness of dual-mode sensors for GSH detection is affected by a variety of experimental conditions. The optimal experimental conditions were explored to obtain the most accurate detection results. The concentration of Cu2+ adsorbed by NSCQDs@MSN was investigated considering the effects of Cu2+ on fluorescence quenching of NSCQDs and oxidizing TMB. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence signal intensity of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu decreased with the increase of adsorbed Cu2+ concentration. In contrast, the absorbance signal increased significantly with the increase of adsorbed Cu2+ concentration, and the trend of the two signal intensities was synthesized to determine the optimal adsorption concentration of Cu2+ as 50 μg/mL. Next, the effects of different pH values of the test solution on the signals of the NSCQDs@MSN-Cu dual-mode sensor were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 3e, both fluorescence and absorbance signals gradually increased and then slightly decreased with the increase of the pH. Therefore, the test solution with the greatest dual-mode signal intensity (pH = 7) was selected as the analytical environment for GSH measurements. Finally, the system concentration of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu was optimized. As shown in Fig. 3f, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu increased gradually in the system concentration of 3 ∼ 11 μg/mL, and then remained basically stable. Therefore, 11 μg/mL was selected as the optimal system concentration of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu for two-mode analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal conditions for Cu2+ adsorption concentration, test solution pH, and NSCQDs@MSN-Cu concentration were determined to be 50 μg/mL, pH = 7, and 11 μg/mL, respectively, based on the results of the above analysis. To assess the sensitivity and detection capabilities of the dual-mode sensor, a range of GSH concentrations were evaluated under the optimal experimental conditions. As depicted in Fig. 4a and b, the fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu continued to recover with the increase of GSH concentration. When the GSH concentration was in the range of 5 ∼ 900 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the dual-mode sensor showed a good linear relationship with its concentration, and the linear regression equation was F = 17.34 C (μM) + 10340 (R2 = 0.9962). As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the absorbance signal of the dual-mode sensor at 652 nm continued to diminish with increasing GSH concentration in the range of 20 ∼ 460 μM, along with excellent linearity, with a linear regression equation of F = −0.001015 C + 0.5633 (R2 = 0.9954). These two signal modes were found to have detection limits of 1.6 μM and 7 μM, respectively, based on the limit of detection formula (LOD = 3σ/k). When compared to other reported GSH detection methods (as summarized in Table 2), this designed dual-mode sensing system demonstrated a significantly broader linear detection range and enhanced sensitivity. Furthermore, compared with the single-signal mode GSH detection method, the dual-mode sensor designed in this experiment adopts a dual-signal strategy to effectively improve the accuracy and reliability of detection, which has higher practical application value.

In addition, we converted the constructed colorimetric method to a smartphone-based sensing platform using GSH detection. As shown in Fig. 5a, the color of the detection system changed from dark to light and finally approached transparency with the increase of GSH concentration. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 5b, a smartphone was used to take photos of the measurement system with different GSH concentrations (white balance and photosensibility were set to daylight, 300, respectively), and the app was used to obtain the color intensity RGB values of the photos. The color intensity was analyzed according to the formula Y = 1.165 C + 36.6 (R2 = 0.976). There was a good linear relationship between the Y value and GSH concentration in the GSH assay range of 15 ∼ 100 μM, with a calculated LOD value of 8.3 μM (Fig. 5c). To realize the simultaneous processing of multiple sets of data, we have written a program to export multiple sets of results simultaneously (Fig. S2), both to realize the processing of RGB values for a large number of images and, at the same time, to calculate the GSH concentrations represented by different images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the dual-mode sensing system for GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the sensor is a key factor in ensuring the accuracy of detection. To investigate the anti-interference ability of this dual-mode sensor against GSH, a variety of possible coexisting non-target interfering substances were introduced into the detection system. As shown in Fig. 4c and f, only the addition of GSH caused significant changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance intensity, while other non-target interfering substances (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, AA, Ala, Cys, and Glu) had a negligible effect on the dual-mode sensor under the same detection conditions. This shows that the specificity and anti-interference ability of the developed dual-mode sensor for GSH detection are good.

Furthermore, stability is another important index to evaluate whether the sensor has practical application value. Five sets of sensors were prepared under the same conditions, and after 9 days of storage in a 4°C environment, their detection performance could still maintain 90.8% of the original effect, indicating that the dual-mode sensor has good stability (Fig. 4g).

To evaluate the usefulness of the proposed dual-mode sensor for practical applications, experiments were conducted to detect GSH content using 4T1, CT26, and A549 cell lysate as real samples. The results are shown in Table 3 and S1. The concentrations of GSH in the 4T1 cell lysate were 266.5 μM and 251.2 μM by fluorescence and colorimetric methods, respectively. the above two assays were close to each other, which demonstrated that the constructed dual-mode sensors had excellent accuracy and stability. In addition, the recoveries of GSH were determined by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 3, the spiked recoveries of colorimetric and fluorescence measurements ranged from 98.4% to 104.2%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of the assays were in the range of 0.6–8.1%. All these indicators show that the requirements for quantitative detection of GSH content in complex samples are satisfied. The above experimental results indicate that the designed dual-mode sensor exhibits good accuracy and reliability with exceptional analytical performance.

In summary, a dual-mode sensor (denoted as NSCQDs@MSN-Cu) based on MSN was successfully developed. This sensor exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, making it a valuable tool for the accurate quantification of GSH. The in-situ synthesis of NSCQDs in MSN not only mitigated the aggregation effect of NSCQDs, but also resulted in a notable enhancement the fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs. Moreover, the mesoporous structure of NSCQDs@MSN-Cu could selectively enrich the target on the surface, thus amplifying the dual-mode signal and greatly improving the sensitivity of detection. Following the optimization of conditions for Cu2+ adsorption concentration, test solution pH, and NSCQDs@MSN-Cu concentration, the performance of the dual-mode sensor was evaluated. The experimental construction of this sensor yielded fluorescence and absorbance signals that demonstrated strong linear correlations with the concentration of GSH. Impressively, the sensor achieved a detection limit as low as 1.6 μM. These findings affirmed the sensor's capability for high-stability and high-specificity detection of GSH, employing a sophisticated fluorescence-colorimetric dual-signal recognition strategy. Therefore, this dual-mode strategy provided a novel and promising avenue for the development of high-performance nanosensors for multifunctional sensing, which holds significant potential for applications in biochemical research and clinical diagnosis, opening up new horizons in these fields.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 82372100, 32000991), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2023M732602), the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by Tianjin (No. TJSQNTJ-2020–02), and the Key R&D Program of Hunan Province (No. 2021SK2035).

Sheng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaoqun Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Shutong Chen: Investigation. Huan Li: Investigation. Chen Cao: Investigation. Jiansen Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile Construction of Biomimetic Nanosensors for Electrochemical Monitoring of Intracellular Glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.T. Wu, X. Chen, Y.T. Jiao, W.T. Fan, Y.L. Liu, W.H. Huang, Versatile Construction of Biomimetic Nanosensors for Electrochemical Monitoring of Intracellular Glutathione, Angewandte Chemie International Edition 61(15) (2022) e202115820.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive and rapid detection of glutathione based on a fluorescence-enhanced “turn-on” strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Ma, M. Wang, H. Cai, F. Li, S. Fu, Y. Liu, Y. Zhao, A sensitive and rapid detection of glutathione based on a fluorescence-enhanced “turn-on” strategy, Journal of Materials Chemistry B 9(16) (2021) 3563-3572.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “One stone, five birds”: Ultrabright and multifaceted carbon dots for precise cell imaging and glutathione detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, K.-F. Xu, G. Wang, S. Durrani, F. Lin, F.-G. Wu, “One stone, five birds”: Ultrabright and multifaceted carbon dots for precise cell imaging and glutathione detection, Chemical Engineering Journal 457 (2023) 140997.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Glutathione Activatable Ion Channel Induces Apoptosis in Cancer Cells by Depleting Intracellular Glutathione Levels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Yang, J. Liu, W. Hu, Y. Xiao, H. Chen, Y. Long, H. Zheng, Nano-ferroelectric oxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of glutathione, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 393 (2023) 134170.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiansen Li received his Master’s degree from Shandong University of Technology in 2022. He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in Biology with the School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University. His current research interest is developing drug delivery systems and electrochemical biosensor.

Chen Cao received his Master’s degree from Lanzhou University in 2021. He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in Biology with the School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University. His current research interest is developing functional fluorescent agents.

Huan Li received her B.S. degree from China University of Mining and Technology in 2021. She is currently working toward the Master’s degree in Biology with the School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University. Her current research interest is developing drug delivery systems for tumor therapy.

Shutong Chen received her B.S. degree from Henan University of Technology in 2021. She is currently working toward the Master’s degree in Biology with the School of Life Sciences, Tianjin University. Her current research interest is developing nanosystems for cancer gas therapy.

Xiaoqun Gong received her Ph.D. in materials science from Tianjin University in 2013. Then she joined the faculty of Tianjin University. Her current research interest is biosensors and controlled drug release.

Sheng Wang received his Ph.D. in materials science from Tianjin University in 2015. He then worked as a postdoctoral researcher at National Institutes of Health (NIH). In 2019, he joined the faculty of Tianjin University as a Professor. His current research focuses on the design and development of stimuli-responsive nanomaterials for biomedical applications.
